description: Positions in TCOM, NET, CRM, CACI, PDD, and GOOGL were all entered on thematic or sector narratives (China recovery, AI integration, government IT momentum) without a confirmed near-term binary event such as an earnings release, contract award, or product announcement within the holding window. The trade thesis was directionally plausible in each case but structurally mismatched to a 10-day swing hold period. This pattern produced losses ranging from 2.58% to 16% and was the single most repeated failure mode across both Core and Swing portfolios.
recommendation: Implement a mandatory pre-entry checklist requiring at least one confirmed near-term catalyst (earnings date, contract announcement, or analyst event) scheduled within the hold window before any swing position is opened; if no catalyst exists within 10 days, classify the idea as a watchlist candidate only.

description: TCOM, CIHKY, IDCBY, and PDD were all held simultaneously as separate positions but shared near-identical risk factors including China macro weakness, ADR regulatory overhang, and US-China geopolitical risk premium, creating a hidden single-country concentration that amplified portfolio-level drawdown when China sentiment turned risk-off. The losses across these four names were correlated rather than independent, negating any diversification benefit assumed by treating them as separate holdings. Classifying CIHKY and IDCBY as Core positions further compounded the damage by anchoring them to a longer hold mandate.
recommendation: Enforce a hard rule capping total exposure to any single foreign country across all portfolios at 15% of combined portfolio value, and require that no more than one China-related position be held in the Core portfolio at any time given the elevated regulatory and delisting risk specific to that market.

pattern: Wrong Portfolio Classification for Volatile or Illiquid Assets
description: ASGN, CIHKY, and IDCBY were each labeled as Core holdings despite carrying characteristics fundamentally incompatible with that designation, including high cyclicality, OTC illiquidity, thin US trading volume, and sovereign regulatory risk. Core classification implies stability and lower monitoring frequency, but these names required active risk management that the classification discouraged. Misclassification delayed recognition of deteriorating conditions and made early exits psychologically harder to execute.
recommendation: Establish explicit quantitative criteria for Core eligibility requiring a minimum average daily US trading volume of $10M, primary listing on a major US exchange (not OTC), and a beta below 1.3 measured over the trailing 90 days; any position failing these thresholds must be reclassified as Speculative regardless of the underlying thesis.

description: META and GOOGL both demonstrated the value of rapid loss-cutting, with exits at 4 days and 3 days limiting losses to 2.2% and 2.58% respectively, while positions held the full 10-day window in the same batch suffered losses of 6% to 19%. This inconsistency reveals that the trader possessed the behavioral capability to exit quickly but applied it selectively rather than systematically, allowing avoidable losses to compound in other names. The pattern suggests stop-loss rules existed conceptually but were not operationalized at the time of entry.
recommendation: Set a hard stop-loss order at exactly 3.5% below entry price for every swing position at the moment the order is filled, without exception, so that early-exit discipline is mechanical and automatic rather than discretionary and emotionally variable.

pattern: High-Multiple or Illiquid Stocks Amplifying Macro Sensitivity
description: NET, CRM, and IDCBY each suffered outsized losses relative to broader market moves because their valuations or liquidity profiles made them disproportionately sensitive to macro regime shifts such as rising rate expectations, risk-off sentiment, or China credit repricing. High-revenue-multiple growth stocks like NET and CRM compress sharply when market discount rates rise even modestly, while illiquid ADRs like IDCBY widen spreads and gap down with minimal selling pressure. Entering these names without accounting for their macro amplification factor understated true position risk.
recommendation: Before entering any stock with a price-to-sales ratio above 10x or average daily volume below $5M in US markets, require a written macro regime check confirming that VIX is below 20 and the relevant sector ETF is trending above its 20-day moving average, and reduce intended position size by 50% if either condition is not met.
